summary: Drew DeVault offers himself as a personal resource to anyone working in free software, inviting readers to reach out for feedback, advice, and collaboration. He acknowledges that his blog posts have inspired people to start FOSS businesses and take on community leadership roles. He lists specific ways people have already engaged with him, from pitching ideas to seeking philosophical debate. The post emphasizes that the free software movement depends on mutual support and mentorship, not individual effort alone.
key_points: DeVault explicitly invites one-on-one engagement beyond his public blog writing
He frames FOSS success as a collective effort requiring people to rely on each other
He lists concrete ways readers can engage: pitching ideas, seeking feedback on drafts, asking for community advice
He acknowledges having his own confidants, normalizing the need for mentorship and peer support
money_quotes: I'm sitting here on my soapbox shouting into the void, but I also want to work with you one-on-one.
None of us goes it alone, and for this great FOSS experiment to succeed, we need to rely on each other.
I want to be personally available to you.
Please consider me a resource for your work in FOSS.
It makes me proud to see the difference you're making, and I'm honored to have inspired many of you.
key_insight: The free software movement succeeds through personal mentorship and mutual support, not just public advocacy.
